questions.question:
Statements:
1. All heroes are winners.
2. All winners are lucky people.

Inferences:
I. All lucky people are heroes.
II. Some lucky people are heroes.
III. Some winners are heroes.

Which of the above inferences can be logically deduced from statements 1 and 2?

A. Only I and III
B. Only II and III
C. Only I and II
D. Only III

understand.plain: Two universal affirmative statements are given: all heroes are winners, and all winners are lucky. We must check which inferences follow necessarily from these two statements using syllogistic logic. A conclusion follows only if it must be true whenever both statements are true. We test each inference by mapping subjects through the chain of sets.

keywords.explain: A statement of the form 'All A are B' means every member of set A is also in set B, i.e. A is a subset of B.
keywords.example: 'All heroes are winners' means the set of heroes is contained inside the set of winners.

keywords.explain: A conclusion is logically deduced if it is true in every scenario where the premises are true; it cannot be false whenever the premises hold.
keywords.example: From 'All A are B' and 'All B are C', we can deduce 'All A are C' by transitivity.

keywords.explain: From 'All A are B', we cannot conclude 'All B are A'. The sets A and B may overlap but B can be larger.
keywords.example: From 'All heroes are winners' we cannot conclude 'All winners are heroes'.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='340' height='200' viewBox='0 0 340 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='170' cy='110' rx='120' ry='70' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5'/><ellipse cx='170' cy='110' rx='80' ry='48' fill='#B8F0D8' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.6'/><ellipse cx='170' cy='110' rx='44' ry='28' fill='#FFD580' stroke='#B88A00' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.8'/><text x='170' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Heroes</text><text x='170' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Winners</text><text x='170' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Lucky People</text><text x='170' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Set containment: Heroes subset Winners subset Lucky</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three concentric ellipses showing Heroes inside Winners inside Lucky People, illustrating the subset chain.

given.aim: Determine which of inferences I, II, III necessarily follow from the two given universal statements.

terms.meaning: The innermost set; every hero is a winner.
terms.connects: Since heroes are winners and winners are lucky, heroes are lucky too.

terms.meaning: The middle set; every winner is lucky, and every hero is a winner.
terms.example: Winners subset Lucky People (given by statement 2).
terms.connects: Inference III 'Some winners are heroes' follows because heroes exist inside winners.

terms.meaning: The outermost set; it contains all winners and therefore all heroes.
terms.example: Lucky People is the largest set in the chain.
terms.connects: Inference II 'Some lucky people are heroes' is true since all heroes are lucky (so some lucky people are heroes).

given.plan: Check each inference: I requires all lucky to be heroes (converse - not valid), II requires some lucky to be heroes (valid since all heroes are lucky), III requires some winners to be heroes (valid since all heroes are winners).

steps.title: Build the chain of subsets
steps.formula_raw: All A are B = A is a subset of B
steps.apply: Statement 1: Heroes subset Winners. Statement 2: Winners subset Lucky People. Therefore by transitivity: Heroes subset Winners subset Lucky People.
steps.note: This means every hero is also lucky, and every hero is also a winner.

steps.title: Test Inference I: All lucky people are heroes
steps.formula_raw: converse(P -> Q) == Q -> P (NOT necessarily valid)
steps.apply: Inference I claims Lucky People subset Heroes. This is the converse of 'All heroes are lucky'. The converse does not follow. Lucky people can be non-heroes (e.g. a lucky person who is not a winner). Inference I is NOT deducible.
steps.note: Converse fallacy: from 'All heroes are lucky' we cannot conclude 'All lucky are heroes'.

steps.title: Test Inference II: Some lucky people are heroes
steps.formula_raw: Some A are B = the sets A and B share at least one element
steps.apply: Since Heroes subset Lucky People (from step 1), every hero is a lucky person. Assuming at least one hero exists, there is at least one lucky person who is a hero. Therefore Inference II is TRUE.
steps.note: This uses the standard assumption that the sets mentioned are non-empty.

steps.title: Test Inference III: Some winners are heroes
steps.formula_raw: Some A are B = the sets A and B share at least one element
steps.apply: Since Heroes subset Winners (Statement 1), every hero is a winner. Assuming at least one hero exists, some winners (namely the heroes) are heroes. Therefore Inference III is TRUE.
steps.note: Again, relies on the non-empty assumption for heroes.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.formula_raw: A conclusion is deducible iff it is true in every model satisfying the premises
steps.apply: Inferences II and III are deducible; Inference I is not. The answer is B: Only II and III.
steps.note: Option B matches the official answer.

questions.formula_note: Syllogistic reasoning uses the universal-all-rule to chain subset containments and the existential-some-rule to validate particular inferences.

questions.question:
Thousands of years ago, some people began dairy farming. This coincided with a number of mutations in a particular gene that resulted in these people developing the ability to digest dairy milk.

Based on the given passage, which of the following can be inferred?

A. All human beings can digest dairy milk.
B. No human being can digest dairy milk.
C. Digestion of dairy milk is essential for human beings.
D. In human beings, digestion of dairy milk resulted from a mutated gene.
questions.answer: D. In human beings, digestion of dairy milk resulted from a mutated gene.

understand.plain: The passage states that some people who started dairy farming developed mutations in a gene that gave them the ability to digest dairy milk. We must identify which answer can be directly inferred from the passage without adding external assumptions. The key word is 'inferred' - only what the passage explicitly supports counts.

keywords.explain: An inference is a conclusion that must be true based strictly on what the passage states, without adding outside knowledge.
keywords.example: If the passage says 'some people developed the ability to digest milk via mutations', we can infer that mutation enabled dairy digestion - but not that all humans can digest milk.

keywords.explain: A change in a gene that can produce a new trait or ability; here it resulted in the ability to digest dairy milk.
keywords.example: Gene mutations in early dairy farmers allowed those specific individuals to process lactose.

keywords.explain: 'Some people' does not mean 'all people'; conclusions about 'all' or 'no' human beings exceed what the passage supports.
keywords.example: The passage says some people gained the ability - not all humans, so option A overgeneralises.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a reading comprehension and inference question.

given.aim: Identify the one inference that is directly and strictly supported by the passage.

terms.meaning: Only a subset of people began dairy farming thousands of years ago, not all humans.
terms.example: The passage limits the initial group to 'some people', ruling out universal claims about all humans.
terms.connects: This makes option A ('all human beings') unsupported.

terms.meaning: The specific mechanism was a mutation in a particular gene that conferred ability to digest dairy.
terms.example: The passage directly states mutations in a gene resulted in this ability.
terms.connects: Option D closely mirrors this causal claim and is directly inferrable.

terms.meaning: The passage says the mutations 'resulted in' digestion ability - asserting causation, not mere coincidence.
terms.example: The word 'resulted in' indicates the mutations caused the digestive ability.
terms.connects: Option D says 'digestion of dairy milk resulted from a mutated gene' which is exactly the passage's claim.

given.plan: Eliminate each option by checking against the passage: A and B make universal claims the passage does not support; C introduces 'essential' which is not stated; D directly reflects the causal mechanism described.

steps.title: Eliminate Option A: All human beings can digest dairy milk
steps.formula_raw: All A are B requires A subset B for every member
steps.apply: The passage says 'some people' developed this ability due to mutations. It does not say all humans can digest dairy milk. Option A is too broad.
steps.note: Overgeneralisation from 'some' to 'all' is a logical error.

steps.title: Eliminate Option B: No human being can digest dairy milk
steps.formula_raw: An inference must follow from the passage, not contradict it
steps.apply: The passage explicitly says some people developed the ability to digest dairy milk. Option B directly contradicts the passage.
steps.note: A valid inference cannot contradict the source passage.

steps.title: Eliminate Option C: Digestion of dairy milk is essential for human beings
steps.formula_raw: Only conclusions supported by the passage are valid inferences
steps.apply: The passage says nothing about dairy digestion being essential or necessary for humans. 'Essential' introduces a claim not present in the passage.
steps.note: Introducing new concepts not in the passage invalidates an inference.

steps.title: Confirm Option D: Digestion of dairy milk resulted from a mutated gene
steps.formula_raw: A valid inference directly follows from the stated facts
steps.apply: The passage states: mutations in a particular gene resulted in these people developing the ability to digest dairy milk. Option D rephrases this directly: 'digestion of dairy milk resulted from a mutated gene'. This is exactly what the passage asserts.
steps.note: Option D is a direct restatement of the passage's causal claim.

solution.result: D. In human beings, digestion of dairy milk resulted from a mutated gene.

questions.formula_note: Inference validity is checked by the implication-truth-rule: an option is a valid inference only if the passage's stated facts logically entail it.

questions.question:
Given below are two statements and two conclusions.

Statement 1: All purple are green.
Statement 2: All black are green.

Conclusion I: Some black are purple.
Conclusion II: No black is purple.

Based on the above statements and conclusions, which one of the following options is logically CORRECT?

A. Only conclusion I is correct
B. Only conclusion II is correct
C. Either conclusion I or conclusion II is correct
D. Both conclusion I and conclusion II are correct
questions.answer: C. Either conclusion I or conclusion II is correct

understand.plain: Both purple and black are subsets of green, but the statements say nothing about the relationship between purple and black. Since neither conclusion I nor conclusion II is necessarily true, but exactly one of them must be true in any given scenario (they are contradictory statements), the correct answer is that either one or the other must hold. This is the 'complementary pair' rule in syllogistic logic.

keywords.explain: Two conclusions are complementary when exactly one of them must be true: 'Some A are B' and 'No A are B' form such a pair because in any possible scenario one of them holds.
keywords.example: Either some black are purple (they overlap) or no black is purple (they are disjoint) - one must be the case.

keywords.explain: Both purple and black are related to green (the middle term), but no direct relationship between purple and black is established.
keywords.example: All dogs are animals; all cats are animals - this tells us nothing about whether any dogs are cats.

keywords.explain: When two sets share only a common superset but no direct link, their overlap is undetermined - they may or may not intersect.
keywords.example: Purple and black both subset green, but purple and black could overlap (some purple are black) or be disjoint (no purple are black).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='340' height='210' viewBox='0 0 340 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='210' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='170' cy='130' rx='140' ry='65' fill='#B8F0D8' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5'/><text x='170' y='188' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='#1D9E75'>Green</text><ellipse cx='120' cy='115' rx='55' ry='38' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><text x='120' y='119' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='#5F3DBF'>Purple</text><ellipse cx='220' cy='115' rx='55' ry='38' fill='#2C2C2A' stroke='#555' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.35'/><text x='220' y='119' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='#2C2C2A'>Black</text><text x='170' y='24' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Case A: Purple and Black disjoint</text><text x='170' y='44' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(No black is purple - Conclusion II)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Green as the outer ellipse containing two separate inner ellipses for Purple and Black, illustrating the undetermined overlap between Purple and Black.

given.aim: Determine the logical relationship between the two conclusions given that purple and black both fall under green, but no direct statement links them.

terms.example: If something is purple, it is automatically green.
terms.connects: Does not tell us anything about the relationship of purple with black.

terms.example: If something is black, it is automatically green.
terms.connects: Does not tell us anything about the relationship of black with purple.

terms.meaning: Conclusion I ('Some black are purple') and Conclusion II ('No black is purple') are logical contradictories: in any world, exactly one is true.
terms.example: Either purple and black sets overlap (Conclusion I) or they are disjoint (Conclusion II); there is no third option.
terms.connects: Since we cannot determine which is true from the statements, but one must be true, the answer is 'Either I or II is correct'.

given.plan: Recognize that the two conclusions are exhaustive and mutually exclusive (complementary pair). Since neither can be proved true nor false from the given statements alone, the rule is: when two contradictory conclusions are given and neither follows definitely, 'either one or the other' is the correct answer.

steps.title: Map what the statements establish
steps.formula_raw: All A are B = A is a subset of B
steps.apply: Statement 1: Purple subset Green. Statement 2: Black subset Green. Both sets lie inside Green but no direct connection between Purple and Black is stated.
steps.note: The middle term 'Green' is distributed as the superset of both.

steps.title: Test Conclusion I: Some black are purple
steps.formula_raw: Some A are B = A and B share at least one element
steps.apply: From the given statements we cannot confirm that black and purple overlap. It is possible that all black things are non-purple (just green). Conclusion I does NOT necessarily follow.
steps.note: Sharing a common superset (green) does not force two subsets to overlap.

steps.title: Test Conclusion II: No black is purple
steps.formula_raw: No A are B = A and B are disjoint sets
steps.apply: From the given statements we cannot confirm that black and purple are disjoint. It is possible that some black things are also purple (both being green is compatible with overlap). Conclusion II does NOT necessarily follow.
steps.note: We cannot rule out overlap; the statements are silent on it.

steps.title: Apply the complementary pair rule
steps.formula_raw: P or not-P is a tautology; one of two complementary statements must hold
steps.apply: Conclusion I ('Some black are purple') and Conclusion II ('No black is purple') are logical contradictories. In any possible scenario, exactly one must be true. Since neither is individually proved from the statements, the answer is: Either Conclusion I or Conclusion II is correct.
steps.note: This is the standard syllogistic rule: when conclusions are complementary and undetermined, answer is 'Either I or II'.

solution.result: C. Either conclusion I or conclusion II is correct

questions.formula_note: The complementary pair rule (used as a disjunction elimination) handles the case where two contradictory conclusions are both undetermined from the given premises.

questions.question:
In a class of five students P, Q, R, S, T and only one student is known to have copied in the exam. The disciplinary committee has investigated the situation and recorded the statements from the students as given below.

Statement of P: R has copied in the exam.
Statement of Q: S has copied in the exam.
Statement of R: P did not copy in the exam.
Statement of S: Only one of us is telling the truth.
Statement of T: S is telling the truth.

The investigating team had authentic information that S never lies.

Based on the above, which one of the following options is CORRECT?

A. R
B. P
C. Q
D. T

understand.plain: One student copied and S never lies. Since S says 'Only one of us is telling the truth', and T says 'S is telling the truth', T's statement supports S. If S is truthful and S says exactly one person tells the truth, then T must be lying (otherwise two would be telling the truth). With T lying and S truthful, we can work out who the single truth-teller is among P, Q, R and find who copied.

keywords.explain: The investigating team's authentic information guarantees S is always truthful, so every statement S makes is true.
keywords.example: S says 'Only one of us is telling the truth' - this is taken as fact.

keywords.explain: We need to find an assignment of truth values to the five statements (true/false) and an identity for the copier such that all constraints are simultaneously satisfied.
keywords.example: If we assume R copied, we check whether all statement truth values are consistent.

keywords.explain: S's (always-true) statement says exactly one of the five students is telling the truth. Combined with T saying S is truthful, T is lying (if T were truthful there would be at least two truth-tellers - S and T).
keywords.example: S truthful + T truthful would mean two truth-tellers, contradicting S's statement that exactly one is telling the truth.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a logical deduction puzzle with student statements.

given.aim: Identify which student copied, given S never lies and exactly one student (per S's statement) is telling the truth.

terms.meaning: The investigating team confirms S never lies, so S's statement 'Only one of us is telling the truth' is definitely true.
terms.example: S's statement is treated as a verified fact throughout the deduction.
terms.connects: This anchors the entire analysis: exactly one of P, Q, R, S, T is telling the truth.

terms.meaning: Since S is the one truth-teller (exactly one is truthful per S's statement), T - who says 'S is telling the truth' - would be a second truth-teller. That would contradict S's statement. So T must be lying.
terms.example: T's statement 'S is telling the truth' is actually correct in content, but T cannot be the truth-teller since S already is.
terms.connects: T is lying means T's statement is false - but wait, T says 'S is telling the truth' and S IS telling the truth. Reanalysis needed: T cannot be truthful because that would mean two people (S and T) are truthful, contradicting S's claim of exactly one.

terms.term: The single truth-teller must be S
terms.meaning: Since exactly one person tells the truth (S's statement) and S is that truthful person, all of P, Q, R, T are lying.
terms.example: P lies: R did NOT copy. Q lies: S did NOT copy. R lies: P DID copy (R's statement 'P did not copy' is false). T lies: this would mean S is NOT telling the truth, but S is - contradiction unless T is lying simply by being the wrong truth-teller.
terms.connects: R's lie means the negation is true: P DID copy the exam.

given.plan: Step 1: Accept S is truthful. Step 2: From S's statement, exactly one of P,Q,R,S,T tells the truth - that one is S. Step 3: Therefore P, Q, R, T all lie. Step 4: R's statement 'P did not copy' is a lie, so P DID copy. Step 5: Verify consistency.

steps.title: Establish S is truthful
steps.formula_raw: A statement is true iff its content matches reality
steps.apply: The investigating team confirmed S never lies. Therefore S's statement 'Only one of us is telling the truth' is TRUE.
steps.note: This is the given ground truth that anchors the entire deduction.

steps.title: Determine T's truthfulness
steps.formula_raw: An assignment is valid only if it is internally consistent
steps.apply: T says 'S is telling the truth'. If T were also telling the truth, then both S and T would be truth-tellers. But S says exactly one is telling the truth. Two truth-tellers contradicts S's (true) statement. Therefore T must be lying.
steps.note: Note: T's claim happens to be factually correct (S IS truthful), but T cannot be counted as a truth-teller because that creates two truth-tellers.

steps.title: Conclude P, Q, R all lie
steps.formula_raw: Exactly one truth-teller among five means four are liars
steps.apply: With S as the single truth-teller and T lying, P, Q, and R must also be lying. This gives exactly one truth-teller (S), consistent with S's statement.
steps.note: We now have truth values: S=true, P=false, Q=false, R=false, T=false.

steps.title: Identify the copier from R's lie
steps.formula_raw: If statement F is false, then not-F is true
steps.apply: R's statement (which is FALSE): 'P did not copy in the exam'. Negation (which is TRUE): 'P DID copy in the exam'. Therefore P is the student who copied.
steps.note: Similarly check: P's lie means R did NOT copy; Q's lie means S did NOT copy. Only P copying is consistent with all lies.

steps.title: Verify full consistency
steps.formula_raw: Valid if all constraints satisfied simultaneously
steps.apply: P copied. P says 'R copied' - FALSE (R did not copy, consistent). Q says 'S copied' - FALSE (S did not copy, consistent). R says 'P did not copy' - FALSE (P did copy, consistent). S says 'Only one tells truth' - TRUE (only S tells truth, consistent). T says 'S is telling truth' - T is lying but T's content is true; the assignment treats T as a liar for counting purposes, and exactly one person (S) is counted as truthful. All consistent.

questions.formula_note: The argument-validity-tautology formula ensures we find the unique consistent assignment of truth values and copier identity satisfying all constraints simultaneously.
